skill.name: javascript-sdk
skill.description: Guides JavaScript and TypeScript developers using the inference.sh SDK for apps, agents, streaming, files, tools, and server proxies.
skill.summary: Build inference.sh JavaScript and TypeScript integrations with SDK examples for apps, agents, files, and proxies.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, environment, script, and heuristic hits. Review shows most command and weak-crypto hits are markdown documentation examples or terminology, not active malicious code, but the skill does include copy-paste examples with eval, webhooks, server secrets, and browser key guidance that require warnings.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe eval in Tool Handler Examples
medium_findings.description: The tool builder reference evaluates a tool call expression with eval. The context is documentation for a calculator tool, but copying this pattern could execute untrusted model or user-controlled input in an application.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The eval calls are direct and operate on call.args.expression in tool-call handlers. The surrounding text presents them as SDK examples rather than malicious active code, so the risk is real but contextual.

medium_findings.title: Frontend API Key Exposure Pattern
medium_findings.description: The server proxy reference includes a development branch that uses NEXT_PUBLIC_INFERENCE_API_KEY directly in client setup. Public environment variables can expose service credentials if copied into browser builds.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The key name explicitly uses a public frontend prefix and is placed in client initialization. The example labels it development-only, which lowers confidence that exposure is intended.

medium_findings.title: External Webhook Tool Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill documents webhook tools that call Slack, GitHub, and custom HTTP endpoints with tool arguments and stored secrets. This is legitimate SDK functionality, but it can move sensitive data to third-party services if configured without approval and allowlists.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls are explicit and tied to webhook tool examples. No evidence shows malicious destinations or hidden exfiltration, but the capability is sensitive by design.

medium_findings.title: Broad Agent Code Execution Capability
medium_findings.description: The main skill shows internal tools enabling code execution for an agent. This can be legitimate for developer agents, but published guidance should warn users to sandbox execution and require approval for untrusted prompts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly enable codeExecution through internal tools. The finding depends on how downstream users deploy the agent, so confidence is medium-high rather than very high.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: Most Ruby or shell backtick findings are markdown code fences around installation and TypeScript examples. They do not execute by themselves inside the skill content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown fences or type annotations, not shell execution logic. This explains the large external_commands count from static analysis.

low_findings.title: Server-Side Environment Variable Examples
low_findings.description: The process.env findings mostly show standard server-side API key configuration for proxy handlers. This is expected for SDK authentication, but secrets must remain server-only.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples read named API key environment variables and usually place them in server proxy code. The pattern is legitimate, with residual risk if users copy it into client bundles.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Examples Are User-Initiated File Handling
low_findings.description: The filesystem findings are examples for reading, writing, and streaming files for uploads and downloads. They are not hidden file discovery or automatic exfiltration behavior in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited snippets handle explicit image or upload paths selected by the developer. No evidence found of recursive scanning or unauthorized file collection.

dangerous_patterns.title: eval(call.args.expression)
dangerous_patterns.description: Dynamic evaluation of tool arguments is dangerous when tool calls can be influenced by prompts or users. Replace this with a constrained parser or a fixed operation allowlist.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact pattern appears in two tool handling examples. The risk is well understood because eval executes JavaScript, even though this is documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Public Client API Key Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: A browser-facing public API key variable appears in a client initialization example. Use a server proxy for production and avoid embedding service keys in browser bundles.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example uses NEXT_PUBLIC_INFERENCE_API_KEY, which is commonly exposed to client code. The surrounding comment distinguishes production from development.

dangerous_patterns.title: Webhook Tools With Secrets
dangerous_patterns.description: Webhook tools can send tool arguments to external services and can attach stored secrets. Users should restrict endpoints, validate payloads, and require approval for sensitive actions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The snippets demonstrate external HTTP endpoints and Authorization headers from environment secrets. This is legitimate functionality but can become exfiltration if misconfigured.

content.value_statement: JavaScript teams need clear patterns for connecting apps to inference.sh without exposing keys or breaking streaming workflows. This skill provides practical SDK guidance for apps, agents, files, tools, React, and server proxies.
actual_capabilities: Explains installation and authentication for @inferencesh/sdk in Node.js and browser projects.
Shows how to run inference.sh apps with synchronous, async, and streaming task patterns.
Documents agent creation, tool calls, human approval flows, and reusable skill context.
Covers file uploads, generated file downloads, browser File objects, and large file streams.
Provides server proxy examples for Next.js, Express, Hono, Remix, SvelteKit, and related frameworks.
Includes TypeScript typing patterns, React hooks, and frontend integration examples.
limitations: It is documentation and guidance, not a complete SDK implementation.
Some examples require users to supply valid inference.sh API keys and app references.
Security-sensitive examples need production hardening before copy-paste use.
It does not verify third-party webhook endpoints or external service permissions.

use_cases.title: Add AI Features to a Web App
use_cases.description: Use React and proxy examples to add generation, file upload, and streaming responses without placing API keys in client code.

use_cases.title: Build a TypeScript Agent Service
use_cases.description: Create agents with typed tools, server-side secrets, approval gates, and session handling for production workflows.

use_cases.title: Document SDK Adoption Patterns
use_cases.description: Use the reference sections to explain installation, model execution, files, streaming, and framework-specific proxy setup.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic SDK Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to install @inferencesh/sdk and run one inference.sh app from a TypeScript Node.js script.

prompt_templates.title: Add Secure Browser Access
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me add inference.sh to a React app using a server proxy so no API key is exposed in the browser.

prompt_templates.title: Implement Streaming and Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a TypeScript flow that uploads a user file, starts an inference.sh task, and streams progress updates to the UI.

prompt_templates.title: Review an Agent Tool Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my inference.sh agent tool design for unsafe eval, webhook exfiltration, missing approval gates, and exposed environment secrets.

output_examples.input: User asks how to connect a Next.js app to inference.sh safely.
output_examples.output: A concise plan recommending a backend proxy route, server-only API key storage, frontend proxy configuration, and production checks.

output_examples.input: User asks why streaming task updates are not appearing in React.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting checklist covering stream mode, callback handling, state updates, abort handling, and server proxy response behavior.

output_examples.input: User asks whether a calculator tool should use eval.
output_examples.output: A security-focused answer recommending a small expression parser or fixed operation allowlist instead of dynamic JavaScript execution.

best_practices: Keep inference.sh API keys on the server and route browser traffic through a proxy.
Require approval for tools that call external services, write files, or execute code.
Use TypeScript types and validation schemas for tool parameters and app inputs.
anti_patterns: Do not use eval for calculator or tool-call expressions from users or models.
Do not place service API keys in public frontend environment variables.
Do not send raw user files or secrets to webhooks without validation and consent.

faq.question: Does this skill install the JavaScript SDK for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. You still run your own package manager commands in your project.

faq.question: Can I use it with TypeScript?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes TypeScript imports, type annotations, typed tools, and framework examples.

faq.question: Is it safe for browser applications?
faq.answer: It can be safe when you use a server proxy and keep API keys out of browser bundles.

faq.question: Does it support agents and tools?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents agent setup, tool builders, webhook tools, internal tools, approval gates, and tool result handling.

faq.question: Why is the security risk marked medium?
faq.answer: The skill is mostly documentation, but some examples show eval, external webhooks, code execution, and public key patterns.

faq.question: What should I review before production use?
faq.answer: Review key storage, proxy authentication, webhook destinations, file handling, tool approvals, and any code execution settings.
